Matthew chapter 27 presents the crucifixion of Jesus not as a tragic accident but as the deliberate execution of an innocent man—and yet as something God had willed and foretold. The machinery of human evil grinds relentlessly through this chapter: Judas's betrayal, the Sanhedrin's conspiracy, Pilate's cowardice, the crowds' bloodlust, and the soldiers' brutality. Yet Matthew forces us to see something beneath the surface. He places Judas's suicide immediately after his confession of Jesus's innocence, and he interrupts the narrative of Jesus before Pilate to frame those events with Old Testament fulfillment. The thirty pieces of silver—the price of a slave—become the currency of redemption, used to purchase ground for the burial of foreigners. When Pilate declares himself innocent and washes his hands, Jesus stands silent. When offered an easy escape through the Barabbas exchange, the crowd chooses a murderer over the innocent. The point is not that we should sympathize with Pilate or blame only "the Jews"; it is that human judgment, when set against God, reveals itself as fundamentally blind and inverted.

The mockery and crucifixion that follow are stripped of sentiment. Matthew does not soften what happens. Jesus is beaten, crowned with thorns, stripped naked, crucified between criminals, and taunted by everyone—religious leaders, passersby, even the thieves beside Him. The inscribed charge, "King of the Jews," is meant as a joke. But Matthew includes it because it is true. Every mockery unwittingly testifies to His identity: the religious leaders' taunt that He "saved others" but cannot save Himself contains a terrible irony, for it is precisely by not saving Himself that Jesus accomplishes salvation. His refusal to prove His power by descending from the cross is not weakness but faithfulness to His purpose. When darkness covers the land at noon, we are not watching a mere natural event but a cosmic rupture, creation itself responding to what is happening.

The cry of abandonment—"My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?"—demands that we sit with the weight of what Jesus endured. This is not poetic despair but the words of the Psalmist who knew themselves uttered by the Son of God in His suffering. Matthew gives us no explanation, no divine comfort whispered from heaven at this moment. He gives us instead the raw reality of Jesus's anguish, the moment when the Son bore the full force of separation from the Father's face. And then, in the instant of Christ's death, the temple veil tears from top to bottom, the earth shakes, rocks split, tombs open. What seemed like defeat—Jesus yielding up His spirit while still mocked and abandoned—is revealed as the cosmic pivot point. The barrier between God and sinful humanity, symbolized by the veil that only the high priest could pass once a year, is torn away forever. The centurion, the Gentile soldier who had just overseen an execution, recognizes what the religious establishment refused to see: "Truly this was the Son of God."

We live in the wake of Matthew 27, and it demands a response. We cannot read this chapter and remain neutral. These events either happened as Matthew describes, or the entire Christian faith is built on delusion. If they happened, then we must reckon with the fact that we, by our sin, are represented in that crowd that chose Barabbas. We must see that Jesus's refusal to come down from the cross was His greatest victory and His most perfect act of love. And we must understand that His death was not a tragedy that requires our pity but a ransom that purchased our redemption—and that the torn veil stands as an open invitation to approach God directly through His blood.
